analogy: Think of "Agriculture" through the chapter's own opening images: Social and Political Life 46 Unit Three 2024-25 47 Teacher’s Note Students are going to be introduced to the judiciary through this unit. Yet, aspects of this system like the police, the courts etc. are probably something that students are already quite familiar with either... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 26 January 1950, the day India became a, 22 May 1987. These families fought for justice for, GLOSSARY, Agriculture. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Agriculture is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that social and Political Life 46 Unit Three 2024-25

The text explains that 47 Teacher’s Note Students are going to be introduced to the judiciary through this unit.

The text explains that yet, aspects of this system like the police, the courts etc. are probably something that students are already quite familiar with either through the media or perhaps through personal experience.

The text explains that in this unit, the effort is to combine some basic knowledge on the judicial system with more hands-on information on the criminal justice system.

The text explains that chapter 4 covers topics that will be reinforced in the higher classes.

The text explains that the effort while teaching this should be to provide students with a sense of the significant role played by the judiciary in upholding the principles enshrined in the Constitution.

The text explains that chapter 4 discusses the role of the judiciary in upholding the rule of law.

The text explains that five separate though inter-related concepts on the judiciary have been discussed in this Chapter.

The text explains that the reason why the independence of the judiciary is key to its functioning is a complex idea but something that students need to understand.

The text explains that this can be conveyed at a more basic level using examples of different decision-making processes that the student is familiar with.

The text explains that the structure has been illustrated through a case and students should be encouraged to discuss other cases to better understand the working of the judicial process.

The text explains that the last concept of ‘access to justice’ highlights the role of the Public Interest Litigation (PIL) in facilitating justice.

The text explains that it also touches upon the ‘delay’ in providing justice.

The text explains that the student’s growing knowledge of Fundamental Rights should be used while discussing this section.

The text explains that social and Political Life 48 Chapter 4 A glance at the newspaper provides you a glimpse of the range of work done by the courts in this country.

The text explains that but can you think of why we need these courts?

The text explains that to enforce this rule of law, we have a judicial system that consists of the mechanism of courts that a citizen can approach when a law is violated.

The text explains that as an organ of government, the judiciary plays a crucial role in the functioning of India’s democracy.

The text explains that in this chapter, you will find answers to these questions.

The text explains that broadly speaking, the work that the judiciary does can be divided into the following: Dispute Resolution: The judicial system provides a mechanism for resolving disputes between citizens, between citizens and the government, between two state governments and between the centre and state governments.

The text explains that judicial Review: As the final interpreter of the Constitution, the judiciary also has the power to strike down particular laws passed by the Parliament if it believes that these are a violation of the basic structure of the Constitution.

The text explains that upholding the Law and Enforcing Fundamental Rights: Every citizen of India can approach the Supreme Court or the High Court if they believe that their Fundamental Rights have been violated.

The text explains that chapter 4: Judiciary Supreme Court of India https://www.sci.gov.in 2024-25

The text explains that within this judicial system, the politician has the power to appoint and dismiss a judge from his office.

The text explains that although we often hear of rich and powerful people in India trying to influence the judicial process, the Indian Constitution protects against this kind of situation by providing for the independence of the judiciary.

The text explains that this, as you read in Chapter 1, is a key feature of the Constitution.

The text explains that what this means here is that other branches of government – the legislature and the executive – cannot interfere in the work of the judiciary.

The text explains that the courts are not under the government and do not act on their behalf.

The text explains that for the above separation to work well, it is also crucial that all judges in the High Court as well as the Supreme Court are appointed with very little interference from these other branches of government.

The text explains that do you think that any ordinary citizen stands a chance against a politician in this kind of judicial system?

The text explains that q Type of Dispute Example Dispute between centre and the state Dispute between two states Dispute between two citizens Laws that are in violation of the Constitution 2024-25

The text explains that 51 List two reasons why you believe an independent judiciary is essential to democracy.

The text explains that it also plays a crucial role in protecting the Fundamental Rights of citizens because anyone can approach the courts if they believe that their rights have been violated.

The text explains that the structure of the courts from the lower to the highest level is such that it resembles a pyramid.

The text explains that q Chapter 4: Judiciary Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) www.in.undp.org 2024-25

The text explains that in India, we have an integrated judicial system, meaning that the decisions made by higher courts are binding on the lower courts.

The text explains that another way to understand this integration is through the appellate system that exists in India.

The text explains that let us understand what we mean by the appellate system by tracking a case, State (Delhi Administration) vs Laxman Kumar and Others (1985), from the lower courts to the Supreme Court.

The text explains that write two sentences of what you understand about the appellate system from the given case.

The text explains that chapter 4: Judiciary Aizawl (Mizoram) Bench of the Gauhati High Court http://ghcazlbench.nic.in District Courts Complex in Namchi, South Sikkim http://districtcourtsnamchi.nic.in 2024-25

The text explains that social and Political Life 54 What are the Different Branches of the Legal System?

The text explains that 59 Acquit: This refers to the court declaring that a person is not guilty of the crime which he/she was tried for by the court.

The text explains that to Appeal: In the context of this chapter this refers to a petition filed before a higher court to hear a case that has already been decided by a lower court.

The text explains that compensation: In the context of this chapter this refers to money given to make amends for an injury or a loss.

The text explains that eviction: In the context of this chapter this refers to the removal of persons from land/ homes that they are currently living in.

The text explains that violation: In the context of this chapter it refers both to the act of breaking a law as well as to the breach or infringement of Fundamental Rights.

The text explains that the following is a poster made by the Right to Food campaign.

The text explains that read this poster and list the duties of the government to uphold the Right to Food.

The text explains that how does the phrase “Hungry stomachs, overflowing godowns!

The text explains that we will not accept it!!” used in the poster relate to the photo essay on the Right to Food on page 55?

The text explains that chapter 4: Judiciary Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) www.in.undp.org 2024-25

Source: NCERT Class 8 Resources and Development, Chapter 4: Agriculture
